comments.author: @multible
comments.text:
The turquoise color of Tajikistan’s lakes – like the famous Haftkul (Seven Lakes) or Iskanderkul – is usually not due to algae, but rather minerals and geological processes. Here are the primary causes of the distinctive colors and the differences between them:
1. “Rock Flour”Many of the lakes are born from glaciers. As glaciers move, they grind the bedrock into a very fine dust called glacial flour.How it works: These tiny particles are so light that they float in the water instead of sinking to the bottom. When sunlight hits the particles, the red and yellow wavelengths are absorbed, while the blue and green shades are reflected back to your eyes.The difference in color: The higher the concentration of this “flour,” the more milky and light green/turquoise the lake appears. Lakes further down in a system often have fewer particles, making them a deeper blue.
2. Mineral content (Sodium and Calcium)Especially in the lower lakes, such as Lake Nezhigon, the chemical content plays a big role. The water here has a high concentration of dissolved minerals such as sodium and calcium. This can give the water shades ranging from bright turquoise to purple, depending on the light.
3. Light and ShadowThe depth and surrounding rocks also affect your perception of the color:Depth: Deeper water absorbs more light and often looks dark blue or sapphire blue, while shallow areas appear more turquoise.Shade: Lake Soya (which means "shadow") is located between very steep rocks. Because it is in the shade most of the day, its color often appears darker and cooler than the sunlit neighboring lakes.
Algae can sometimes make the water more greenish, but in the cold, mineral-rich mountain lakes of Tajikistan, it is the inorganic sediments that create the almost "artificial" neon-like turquoise color.

replies.author: @tajtajdar7773
replies.text:
Seven Lakes names.
1. Mižgon / Nežegon – Also called Nezhigon, its name means “eyelashes” due to its curved shape. Known for turquoise, blue, and sometimes purple waters.
2. Soja – Translates to “shadow,” referring to limited sunlight in the narrow valley. Waters range from blue to purple.
3. Hušër / Gušor / Izšor – Meaning “vigilance,” possibly linked to venomous snakes in the area.
4. Nofin (umbilical)– Approximately 2 km long and 400 m wide, often with reddish hues.
5. Churdak / Khurdak – Means “small,” it is the smallest of the seven.
6. Marǧuzor / Marguzor – The largest and most famous lake, light blue and scenic.
7. Hazārčašma / Azorčašma – The highest lake at 2,400 m, fed by springs and rivers, somewhat separate from the others. 
 Love from Tajikistan (Aryanam Vaejah/ Iran Shahr) to world. ❤🤍💚🔥💚🤍❤
